ac:rich-text-body.p: Pulsar Timing Observatory Data Products provide high-precision arrival time measurements enabling gravitational wave detection and tests of fundamental physics with neutron stars.

p: Pulsar Timing Observatory Data Products (PT-ODP) consist of high-precision arrival time measurements, timing residuals, and pulse profiles derived from observations of pulsars. These products enable breakthrough science in gravitational waves, tests of general relativity, and neutron star astrophysics.

ul.li: Times of Arrival (TOAs): Measured pulse arrival times at infinite frequency, referenced to a standard clock (typically UTC). TOAs are the fundamental timing observable with uncertainties of microseconds to tens of microseconds depending on pulsar brightness
Timing Residuals: Differences between observed and predicted pulse arrival times after subtracting a timing model, revealing small departures from the regular pulsar spin-down
Pulse Profiles: Average intensity as a function of pulse phase, enabling pulse morphology studies and assessing data quality
Dispersion Measure (DM): Frequency-dependent delay due to free electrons, derived from multi-frequency observations
Dispersion Measure Variations: Changes in DM over time, indicating changes in the ionised interstellar medium
Observation parameters (date, time, frequency, integration time, antenna used)
Timing noise and arrival time uncertainties
RFI flagging and data quality flags
Software versions and processing parameters

p: The SKA Science Data Processor includes a specialised pulsar timing pipeline:

ol.li: Beamforming: SKA data (voltages or visibilities) are beamformed to create single-pixel or multiple-pixel streams at high time resolution
Dedispersion: Dispersive delays are removed using the known DM (or measuring DM if unknown), aligning signals from all frequency channels
Folding: Time-domain data are folded at the pulsar's rotation period, accumulating energy across many pulses
Template Matching: The folded profile is cross-correlated with a high-quality standard template to measure TOA with maximum precision
Timing Residual Calculation: TOAs are compared to a timing model to extract timing residuals for pulsar-specific analysis

p: Pulsar timing measurements require careful calibration:

ul.li: Flux density scale: Normalised using standard calibrators (e.g., 3C 286, Cassiopeia A) for consistency with other timing programmes
Dispersion measure correction: Precision DM measurements from broadband observations
Parallax and proper motion: Measured from observations at different solar orbital positions and epochs
Clock synchronisation: Tied to coordinated universal time (UTC) and leap second conventions
Polarisation calibration: For Faraday rotation and polarisation-dependent effects

h3: Gravitational Wave Detection via Pulsar Timing Arrays
p: The primary science goal for SKA pulsar timing is the detection of low-frequency gravitational waves through pulsar timing arrays (PTAs):

ul.li: What are PTAs?: Arrays of ~100 pulsars with regular timing observations enable detection of gravitational wave backgrounds from supermassive black hole mergers and other sources
Detection signature: Gravitational waves produce a characteristic correlated timing signal across the pulsar array
SKA advantage: SKA sensitivity enables monitoring of many more pulsars with higher precision, significantly increasing PTA detection power

p: High-precision timing tracks changes in pulsar rotation:

ul.li: Measurement of spin-down rate and its variations
Detection of glitches (sudden spin-up events) and post-glitch relaxation
Studies of pulsar braking and magnetic field evolution

p: Binary pulsars and millisecond pulsars enable precision tests of gravitational theory:

ul.li: Orbital decay measurements in binary systems
Relativistic precession and perihelion advance
Constraints on alternative theories of gravity

p: Timing measurements constrain the neutron star equation of state:

ul.li: Mass measurements from orbital dynamics in binary systems
Internal structure and crustal properties through glitch analysis
Constraints on maximum neutron star mass and composition

p: Dispersion measure variations and scattering measurements probe the ionised ISM:

ul.li: Electron density structures and turbulence
Magnetic field measurements via Faraday rotation
Distances to pulsars and interstellar scattering properties
Timing of very faint pulsars previously inaccessible to other instruments
Microsecond-level TOA uncertainties even for distant pulsars
Detection of small timing variations and subtle relativistic effects
Wide frequency range: SKA-Low covers 50–350 MHz, SKA-Mid covers ~0.35–15 GHz, enabling optimised DM correction and scintillation studies
Simultaneous multi-frequency observations: Longer baselines and faster DM variations detectable than with single-frequency observations

p: SKA's large antenna arrays enable simultaneous observations of multiple pulsars or rapid re-targeting, maximising PTA sample size and observation frequency.
Pulsar timing observations produce much smaller data volumes than continuum surveys:

ul.li: Raw beamformed data: Typically 1–10 GB per observation (depending on band and integration time)
Folded products and TOAs: A few MB to tens of MB per observation
High-precision metadata for long-term consistency
Version control for timing models and calibration
Cross-referencing with other observatories (e.g., other PTA programmes)

p: Users can retrieve PT-ODPs from the SKA Archive filtered by:

ul.li: Pulsar name or source catalogue identifier
Observation date or date range
Frequency band
TOA uncertainty threshold
Data quality metrics

p: Standard tools for pulsar timing analysis include:

ul.li: TEMPO2: Pulsar timing analysis and statistical tools
PSRCHIVE: Pulsar data analysis suite for profiles and timing
NANOGrav Pulsar Timing Suite: PTA-specific analysis tools
Python timing libraries: Enterprise, Libstempo, and others for modern analysis workflows

p: The SKA provides containerised versions of these tools and example Jupyter notebooks for new users.
SKA timing data conform to international standards including:

ul.li: TOA format conventions adopted by the International Pulsar Timing Array
Compatibility with IPTA data archives and analysis pipelines
Coordination with other major timing programmes (NANOGrav, EPTA, PPTA)

ac:rich-text-body.p: SKA pulsar timing data can be combined with observations from other major PTA programmes (NANOGrav, EPTA, PPTA) via standard IPTA conventions, enabling global PTA analyses with enhanced sensitivity.
See also Detected Filterbank Observatory Data Product for pulsar search and discovery and Introduction to Observatory Data Products and Advanced Data Products for the full ODP suite.
